heroOverview: Chills without fever — coded R68.83 in ICD-10 — occur when the body triggers shivering and a cold sensation without a measurable rise in core temperature above 38°C. Unlike the rigors that accompany infectious fever, these chills arise from a separate set of mechanisms: autonomic dysregulation, rapid metabolic shifts, medication effects, endocrine imbalance, or anemia. The symptom is more common than most people realise, affecting an estimated 5–10% of patients presenting to primary care with non-specific systemic complaints. Because the absent fever removes the most obvious pointer to infection, clinicians must cast a wider net — from thyroid disease and low blood sugar to drug reactions and anxiety. Most causes are benign and treatable, but the workup matters because chills without fever can occasionally signal early sepsis before the temperature climbs, severe anemia, or an occult malignancy.

keyStats.prevalence: Reported in 5–10% of adults presenting to primary care with unexplained systemic symptoms
keyStats.demographics: Occurs across all age groups; more common in people with anemia, thyroid disease, or anxiety disorders
keyStats.avgAge: No single age peak; postmenopausal women and older adults with chronic illness are frequently affected
keyStats.globalCases: No precise global incidence data; chills are among the most common non-specific systemic complaints worldwide

definition: Chills without fever (ICD-10: R68.83) is the clinical finding of involuntary, episodic shivering or a subjective sensation of cold in the absence of core body temperature elevation to the febrile threshold (≥38.0°C / 100.4°F). Shivering is generated by rapid, oscillating skeletal muscle contractions coordinated by the hypothalamus as a thermoregulatory response; when this response fires without a genuine thermal demand — or when another disease process mimics the hypothalamic signal — the result is chills without a corresponding fever. The symptom spans many body systems and specialist types: a haematologist addresses anaemia-driven chills, an endocrinologist investigates hypothyroidism or adrenal insufficiency, and an internist rules out early sepsis or lymphoma. Because chills can be the herald of a temperature spike that has not yet appeared, re-assessment within hours is appropriate when infection seems plausible. The diagnosis is essentially a symptom code that prompts an underlying-cause search rather than representing a final diagnosis in its own right.

typesClassification.description: Chills that precede a temperature rise by 30–90 minutes, often seen in bacteraemia, malaria, or influenza before the fever becomes measurable.
Hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, and hypoglycaemia all reduce heat generation or dysregulate the thermostat, producing cold intolerance and episodic chills without fever.

Cytokine release from certain chemotherapy agents, IV amphotericin B, blood or platelet transfusions, and some antibiotics can trigger acute rigors without raising core temperature above the febrile threshold.
Moderate-to-severe anaemia reduces oxygen delivery to peripheral tissues; the resulting vasoconstriction and reduced thermogenesis cause persistent cold sensation and shivering episodes.
Autonomic neuropathy, panic disorder, and other conditions that dysregulate the autonomic nervous system can trigger abnormal vasoconstriction with shivering.
Exposure to cold ambient temperatures, prolonged immersion, or rapid re-warming after hypothermia can produce shivering that persists even after core temperature has normalised.

primarySymptoms: Involuntary shivering or trembling of the limbs and trunk, ranging from mild goosebumps to violent whole-body shaking, occurring without a measured fever.
A subjective sensation of cold or feeling frozen even in a warm room, often accompanied by the desire to pile on blankets.
Pallor or visible mottling of the skin as peripheral vessels constrict to retain heat.
Teeth chattering and jaw trembling, reflecting rapid muscle oscillation across the face and neck.
Fatigue or general malaise associated with the energy cost of sustained muscle activity during shivering episodes.
Piloerection (goosebumps) across the arms, legs, and trunk as arrector pili muscles contract.
Headache or a dull, diffuse body ache accompanying the episode, especially when anaemia or an incubating infection is the cause.
A sense of flushing or sweating immediately after the chill episode resolves, as vasodilatation follows the vasoconstriction phase.
Anxiety, palpitations, or a racing heart, particularly in autonomic-mediated or panic-associated episodes.
Nausea or light-headedness, especially when hypoglycaemia or haemodynamic compromise is the underlying driver.
earlyWarningSigns: A single brief shivering episode in a previously well person following a blood transfusion or new medication.
Mild, cold-clammy sweats alternating with cold sensation without any measurable temperature elevation.
Feeling unusually cold after only brief outdoor exposure on a mild day, suggesting developing anaemia or thyroid dysfunction.
A recurring pattern of afternoon chills several times a week, which can be an early sign of lymphoma or subacute infection.
emergencySigns: Vigorous rigors with confusion, rapid heart rate above 100 bpm, low blood pressure, or reduced urine output, which can signal early sepsis even without fever.
Chills in a person who has recently travelled to a malaria-endemic region — malaria must be excluded urgently.
Severe pallor, breathlessness at rest, or chest pain alongside chills, suggesting critical anaemia.
Chills accompanied by a sudden rash, urticaria, or throat tightening during a transfusion or infusion, indicating an acute transfusion or anaphylactic reaction.
Progressive confusion or loss of consciousness during or after a chill episode, which requires immediate evaluation.

causes.description: Bacterial or viral pathogens trigger cytokine cascades (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α) that reset the hypothalamic thermostat upward; shivering begins as the body generates heat to reach the new set-point, often 30–90 minutes before the thermometer catches up to the febrile level.

When haemoglobin falls below approximately 8 g/dL, peripheral oxygen delivery drops, prompting compensatory vasoconstriction; reduced metabolic heat production and altered sympathetic tone produce a persistent cold sensation and episodic shivering independent of temperature regulation.

Thyroid hormones set the basal metabolic rate; deficiency slows cellular thermogenesis, lowers heat production, and reduces the hypothalamic set-point, producing profound cold intolerance and shivering episodes that may occur without a febrile response.

Blood glucose below approximately 3.0 mmol/L triggers an adrenergic stress response with catecholamine release; trembling, sweating, and a cold sensation result from this counterregulatory surge rather than from temperature dysregulation per se.

Cytokines released during transfusion reactions, or by drugs such as amphotericin B, bleomycin, and interferon-based therapies, act directly on hypothalamic prostaglandin receptors, producing acute rigors that may not raise core temperature to the febrile threshold in all patients.

Autonomic arousal during panic episodes drives intense peripheral vasoconstriction, hyperventilation, and skeletal muscle tension; the resulting cold sensation and shivering closely mimic a chill episode but arise from sympathetic nervous system activation rather than thermoregulatory signalling.

Low cortisol impairs the haemodynamic and metabolic response to physiological stress; peripheral circulation becomes sluggish, heat conservation mechanisms are dysregulated, and chills can occur as one component of general adrenal fatigue or crisis.

riskFactors.description: Haemoglobin below 8 g/dL significantly reduces peripheral thermogenesis and is a common treatable cause; iron deficiency, B12 deficiency, and haemolytic anaemia are the leading specific types.
Both hypothyroidism and, less commonly, Hashimoto's thyroiditis produce cold intolerance; TSH testing is among the first steps when chills are unexplained.
Patients on insulin or secretagogues who experience frequent hypoglycaemic episodes are at high risk of adrenergic chill episodes; better glycaemic management reduces them.
Immunocompromised patients receiving cytotoxic drugs or IV antifungal agents are at elevated risk of drug-induced rigors and are also more vulnerable to infection-related pre-febrile chills.
Plasmodium infection causes classic paroxysmal chills, which can occur without fever in early or partially treated malaria; travel history is essential in the evaluation.
Autonomic dysregulation in anxiety disorders is an under-recognised cause; recognition and treatment of the underlying anxiety disorder resolves episodes in most patients.
Older adults have reduced thermogenic reserve, lower muscle mass for heat production, and higher rates of anaemia and thyroid disease, making them disproportionately prone to chills without fever.
Acute transfusion reactions and infusion of certain medications produce cytokine-mediated rigors in approximately 1–2% of transfusion episodes; the risk is higher with unfiltered products.

affectedDemographics: Postmenopausal women (higher rates of hypothyroidism and anaemia)
Adults over 65 with chronic illness and reduced thermogenic reserve
People receiving cancer chemotherapy or immunosuppressive therapy
Individuals with poorly controlled diabetes on insulin
People with anxiety or panic disorder
diagnosisOverview: The evaluation of chills without fever begins with a detailed history: timing, frequency, duration, associated symptoms, recent medications, travel, and any known chronic illness. The clinician must first exclude infection — including early-stage bacteraemia or malaria — because these can present with rigors before the temperature climbs. A full blood count is the single most informative first test: it detects anaemia, elevated white cell count suggesting incubating infection, and abnormal differentials pointing toward leukaemia or lymphoma. Thyroid function tests (TSH and free T4) are ordered when cold intolerance, fatigue, or weight gain accompany chills. Blood glucose, cortisol, and adrenocorticotropic hormone stimulation testing address adrenal and metabolic causes. Blood cultures are drawn when sepsis is possible, even in the absence of fever, because bacteraemia can precede temperature elevation. A peripheral blood smear and malaria rapid antigen test are essential for any returned traveller. In patients on medications known to cause rigors, a temporal correlation with the drug is usually sufficient. Imaging — typically chest X-ray and abdominal ultrasound — is added when lymphoma, an occult abscess, or a solid tumour is on the differential. The key clinical principle is that a single normal temperature does not exclude infection: if chills are new, recurrent, and unexplained, reassessment at intervals with serial temperatures is appropriate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects anaemia, elevated white cell count, or abnormal cell populations that may indicate infection, haematological malignancy, or other systemic disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw from an arm vein; results are typically available within 2–4 hours. The report shows haemoglobin, haematocrit, white cell differential, and platelet count.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies hypothyroidism, the most common endocrine cause of chronic cold intolerance and chills without fever.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results return within a few hours to one day. An elevated TSH with low free T4 confirms primary hypothyroidism.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes hypoglycaemia as a cause of adrenergic trembling and cold sensation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A simple finger-prick or venous blood sample; fasting glucose takes 8 hours of preparation but a random level provides immediate information.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bacteraemia or fungaemia causing pre-febrile rigors, particularly important when chills are abrupt, severe, or associated with haemodynamic change.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two separate blood draws from different sites; each set of bottles is incubated for up to 5 days, though most positives are flagged within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Malaria rapid antigen test and thick/thin blood smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: Essential in any patient with chills who has travelled to an endemic region in the past 3 months, as malaria can present without fever or with cyclical chills.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fingertip blood sample is used for the rapid antigen strip (result in 15–20 minutes); a smear is sent to the laboratory for microscopic confirmation.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum cortisol and ACTH stimulation test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates adrenal insufficiency when chills are accompanied by fatigue, hypotension, hyponatraemia, or known steroid use.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A morning cortisol blood test is taken first; if low or borderline, a short synacthen/cosyntropin stimulation test is performed with two further draws over 60 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of chills without fever is entirely directed at the underlying cause. When the history and initial tests reveal anaemia, correcting haemoglobin — through oral iron supplementation, B12 replacement, or, in severe cases, blood transfusion — typically resolves the chills within weeks. Hypothyroidism is treated with levothyroxine titrated to normalise TSH; cold intolerance usually improves within 4–8 weeks. Hypoglycaemia management focuses on optimising the diabetes regimen to avoid blood glucose nadirs. If a medication is the cause, dose reduction, pre-medication with paracetamol (acetaminophen) or an antihistamine, or switching to an alternative agent resolves infusion-related rigors. For anxiety- and panic-driven chills, a combination of patient education, cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT), and where appropriate pharmacological treatment with an SSRI or SNRI is effective. When the evaluation points toward an incubating infection, empirical antibiotics guided by the likely source are started promptly, especially if the patient is immunocompromised or haemodynamically unstable. Symptomatic warming with additional blankets and warm oral fluids provides immediate comfort, but it is not a substitute for addressing the cause. Patients should avoid self-treating recurrent unexplained chills with paracetamol alone, as this can mask temperature elevation and delay diagnosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Iron supplementation (oral or intravenous)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for iron-deficiency anaemia-related chills; oral ferrous sulfate 200 mg daily for 3 months rebuilds iron stores, while intravenous iron is used when oral absorption is impaired or correction must be rapid.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Haemoglobin typically rises 1–2 g/dL per month with oral iron; chills related to anaemia resolve in approximately 80–90% of patients as haemoglobin normalises.

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine
medicalTreatments.description: Synthetic thyroid hormone replacement for hypothyroidism; dosed to achieve TSH in the normal range, with gradual uptitration especially in older patients or those with cardiac disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom resolution, including cold intolerance and chills, occurs in approximately 85% of hypothyroid patients within 6–8 weeks of reaching the therapeutic dose.

medicalTreatments.name: Optimised diabetes management (insulin adjustment, dietary education)
medicalTreatments.description: Reducing the frequency and depth of hypoglycaemic episodes through dose adjustment, consistent carbohydrate intake, and structured glucose monitoring eliminates hypoglycaemia-driven adrenergic chills.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Structured glucose management programmes reduce hypoglycaemic episodes by 30–50% in randomised trials, with proportional reduction in associated chills.

medicalTreatments.name: Pre-medication for infusion-related rigors (paracetamol ± antihistamine)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral paracetamol 1 g and a first-generation antihistamine (e.g. diphenhydramine 25–50 mg) given 30 minutes before a blood transfusion or amphotericin infusion reduce the incidence of acute rigors.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces transfusion-related febrile non-haemolytic reactions — the type most likely to cause rigors — by approximately 40–50% compared with no pre-medication.

medicalTreatments.name: SSRIs or SNRIs (for anxiety/panic-related chills)
medicalTreatments.description: When panic disorder or generalised anxiety disorder is the driver, an SSRI such as sertraline or escitalopram taken daily reduces the frequency and severity of autonomic surges that produce chill episodes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SSRIs achieve response rates of approximately 60–70% for panic disorder in randomised controlled trials, with associated reduction in autonomic symptoms including chills.

medicalTreatments.name: Empirical antibiotics (when pre-febrile bacteraemia suspected)
medicalTreatments.description: When clinical assessment raises concern for early infection — rigors, haemodynamic instability, recent procedure, or immunosuppression — broad-spectrum antibiotics are started promptly and narrowed once culture results are available.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Early appropriate antibiotic therapy reduces sepsis mortality by approximately 7% per hour of delay; outcomes are markedly better when treatment begins within 1–3 hours of presentation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary iron and B12 optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Increasing consumption of red meat, leafy greens, legumes, and fortified cereals alongside vitamin C (to enhance iron absorption) can correct mild nutritional anaemia without pharmacological supplementation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)
alternativeTreatments.description: MBSR programmes have demonstrated significant reductions in panic and anxiety symptoms in randomised trials, addressing the autonomic dysregulation that produces anxiety-driven chills in some patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Environmental warming strategies
alternativeTreatments.description: During acute episodes, covering with warm blankets and sipping warm fluids promotes comfort and speeds recovery from the vasoconstriction phase, though these are supportive rather than curative.

whySeeSpecialist: A GP or internist should be the first point of contact for chills without fever. Referral to a haematologist is appropriate when anaemia is unexplained or does not respond to iron therapy. An endocrinologist should be involved when hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, or persistent hypoglycaemia is found. If lymphoma or leukaemia enters the differential, a haematology-oncology opinion is needed. For patients in whom infection cannot be excluded and who are immunocompromised, infectious disease specialist involvement is important.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a general internist or GP experienced in systematic evaluation of unexplained systemic symptoms as the first stop. For anaemia-related chills, a haematologist with access to iron studies and bone marrow biopsy if needed is ideal. If an endocrine cause is suspected, seek an endocrinologist who routinely investigates thyroid disorders and adrenal insufficiency. For anxiety-related episodes, a psychiatrist or psychologist with cognitive-behavioural therapy training is most effective.
preventionStrategies: Maintain adequate nutritional iron, B12, and folate through diet or supplementation to prevent anaemia-related chills.
Monitor thyroid function annually if you have a family history of thyroid disease or are over 60, enabling early treatment before symptoms develop.
Keep blood glucose stable through consistent meals, activity, and medication adherence if you have diabetes.
Use malaria prophylaxis when travelling to endemic regions and seek same-day evaluation on returning with chills.
Inform your healthcare provider of all medications — including chemotherapy agents and biologics — that can cause infusion-related rigors, so pre-medication can be planned.
Treat anxiety or panic disorder proactively rather than waiting for episodes to become frequent and disruptive.
lifestyleModifications: Layer clothing and maintain a warm ambient temperature at home if cold intolerance is a regular problem.
Eat regular meals with adequate protein and complex carbohydrates to keep blood glucose stable and support red blood cell production.
Limit alcohol intake, which impairs thermoregulation and can worsen hypoglycaemia in people on insulin.
Practice stress-reduction techniques such as controlled breathing or progressive muscle relaxation to reduce autonomic arousal.
Keep a symptom diary noting timing, duration, and any associated events (meals, medications, exercise) to help clinicians identify patterns.
Stay well hydrated; dehydration impairs peripheral circulation and can worsen the severity of vasoconstrictive episodes.

recommended: Iron-rich foods: red meat, dark poultry, lentils, tofu, fortified breakfast cereals
Vitamin C sources alongside iron-rich meals to enhance non-haem iron absorption
B12-containing foods: eggs, dairy, fish, meat, or fortified plant-based foods for those at risk of B12 deficiency
Regular small meals to prevent blood glucose nadirs in people with diabetes or hypoglycaemic tendencies
Warm herbal teas or broths during or after acute chill episodes for immediate comfort
avoid: Excessive caffeine, which can trigger adrenergic symptoms resembling or worsening chill episodes
Alcohol, which impairs thermoregulation and can cause reactive hypoglycaemia
Long gaps between meals (skipping meals) when hypoglycaemia is a known trigger
High-calcium or high-tannin foods (milk, tea, coffee) immediately alongside iron supplements, as they impair iron absorption

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise improves peripheral circulation, supports red blood cell production, and reduces anxiety — all of which help reduce chills. Aim for 150 minutes per week of moderate activity such as brisk walking, cycling, or swimming once the underlying cause is being treated. During active chill episodes or with severe anaemia (haemoglobin below 8 g/dL), rest and avoid vigorous activity until haemoglobin is corrected. Dress in layers for outdoor exercise to prevent environmental cold from triggering vasoconstrictive episodes.
dailyManagement: Take iron, levothyroxine, or any prescribed treatment at the recommended time each day without skipping doses.
Monitor and record your blood glucose if you have diabetes to identify patterns that lead to hypoglycaemic chill episodes.
Keep a record of chill episodes — time of day, duration, severity, and any preceding event — to share at follow-up appointments.
Check blood pressure and heart rate during an episode if possible; haemodynamic changes suggest a more serious cause.
Seek same-day assessment if a chill episode is accompanied by confusion, breathlessness, or rapid heart rate.
prognosis: The outlook for chills without fever depends entirely on the underlying cause. When anaemia, hypothyroidism, or hypoglycaemia is identified and corrected, chills typically resolve within 4–8 weeks. Infusion-related rigors resolve with pre-medication or agent substitution. Anxiety-related chills respond well to CBT and pharmacotherapy in the majority of patients. The main risk is delayed diagnosis: when chills without fever are the prodrome of bacteraemia or malaria, a missed window for early treatment can allow progression to sepsis or severe organ dysfunction. In patients with no underlying cause found after thorough evaluation, the prognosis is reassuring — isolated chills in an otherwise healthy individual rarely indicate serious pathology. Recurrent unexplained chills that do not respond to empirical management of common causes warrant haematology-oncology evaluation to exclude lymphoma.
recoveryTimeline: Anaemia-related chills begin to improve within 2–4 weeks of starting effective iron or B12 replacement, with full resolution typically by 8–12 weeks as haemoglobin normalises. Hypothyroid chills improve within 4–8 weeks of achieving adequate levothyroxine dosing. Anxiety-related episodes may take 6–12 weeks to respond to pharmacotherapy, though CBT can produce noticeable improvement within 4–6 weeks. Transfusion- and infusion-related rigors resolve within 30–60 minutes of stopping or slowing the infusion and administering pre-medication.
complications: Progression to sepsis: rigors without fever can be the sole initial presentation of bacteraemia; delay in blood cultures and antibiotics raises mortality risk.
Undiagnosed malaria: missed pre-febrile paroxysms in a returned traveller can progress to cerebral malaria or severe haemolysis within days.
Untreated severe anaemia: ongoing haemoglobin decline causes cardiorespiratory compromise, with risk of high-output cardiac failure.
Unrecognised lymphoma: B-symptom chills (recurring night chills and sweats) are a key diagnostic clue; delay in diagnosis permits disease progression.
Adrenal crisis: undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency presenting with chills can deteriorate rapidly to haemodynamic collapse under physiological stress.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dengue always causes high fever with chills; the absence of fever makes dengue unlikely, but early dengue can briefly present with chills before the fever spike appears.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malaria produces paroxysmal chills classically followed by fever and sweats; chills without any subsequent fever are unusual and warrant a blood smear to exclude early or partially treated malaria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaemia causes persistent cold sensation and shivering due to reduced oxygen delivery, not temperature dysregulation; a full blood count distinguishes it from an infectious chill.

confusedWithConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Uhthoff's phenomenon in MS produces transient neurological symptoms with temperature change, but episodic shivering as a standalone symptom is unusual in MS without other neurological signs.

relatedConditions.name: Dengue fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Chills are a cardinal early symptom of dengue; distinguishing pre-febrile dengue from other causes of chills without fever is a common clinical challenge.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Classic malarial rigors are the prototypical example of paroxysmal chills; travel-related chills must always prompt malaria exclusion.

relatedConditions.name: Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Moderate to severe anaemia is among the most common non-infectious causes of chills without fever, correctable with haematinics.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Pneumonia can present with rigors before fever; respiratory symptoms and signs help distinguish it, but chills may precede the full febrile picture.

faqs.question: What causes chills without fever?
faqs.answer: Common causes include anaemia, hypothyroidism, hypoglycaemia, anxiety, medication reactions, and early infection before fever appears. A blood test usually identifies the cause.

faqs.question: Are chills without fever serious?
faqs.answer: They can be. Most causes are benign and treatable, but chills can precede sepsis or malaria before the temperature rises. New or severe chills with rapid heartbeat or confusion need same-day assessment.

faqs.question: Can anaemia cause chills without fever?
faqs.answer: Yes. When haemoglobin is significantly low, reduced oxygen delivery to tissues causes vasoconstriction and shivering. Correcting anaemia with iron or B12 supplementation typically resolves the chills within 8–12 weeks.

faqs.question: Does hypothyroidism cause chills?
faqs.answer: Yes. Low thyroid hormone slows metabolism and reduces heat production, causing persistent cold intolerance and episodic chills. Levothyroxine replacement resolves symptoms in about 85% of patients within 6–8 weeks.

faqs.question: Can anxiety cause chills without fever?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intense autonomic arousal during panic or anxiety episodes drives peripheral vasoconstriction and muscle trembling that closely mimic an infectious chill. CBT and SSRIs are effective treatments.

faqs.question: Should I see a doctor for chills without fever?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially if they recur. A full blood count and thyroid function test identify the most common causes quickly. Seek same-day care if chills are severe, associated with rapid heart rate, or you have recently travelled to a malaria-endemic area.

faqs.question: Can chills without fever be a sign of cancer?
faqs.answer: Rarely, but recurrent unexplained chills alongside night sweats and unintended weight loss can be a B-symptom of lymphoma. If no common cause is found after standard testing, haematology referral is appropriate.

faqs.question: What blood tests are done for chills without fever?
faqs.answer: The core panel is a full blood count, thyroid function tests, and blood glucose. Blood cultures are added if infection is possible. A malaria test is done for any returned traveller.

faqs.question: Can a blood transfusion cause chills?
faqs.answer: Yes. Febrile non-haemolytic transfusion reactions cause rigors in about 1–2% of transfusions. Pre-medication with paracetamol and an antihistamine reduces the risk by roughly 40–50%.

faqs.question: How long do chills without fever typically last?
faqs.answer: Duration depends on the cause. Infusion-related rigors resolve within 30–60 minutes. Hypoglycaemic chills ease as glucose is restored. Anaemia and thyroid-related chills recur until the underlying condition is treated.

faqs.question: Can malaria present with chills but no fever?
faqs.answer: Yes. Early or partially treated malaria can show the chill phase without measurable fever. Any returned traveller with chills should have a malaria rapid antigen test and blood smear regardless of temperature.

faqs.question: Is chills without fever the same as rigors?
faqs.answer: Rigors are an intense form of chills — violent, uncontrollable shaking. Chills are more general and can be mild. Both can occur without fever. Rigors tend to suggest a more serious underlying cause and need urgent evaluation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between chills with fever and chills without fever?
faqs.answer: Chills with fever almost always point to infection. Chills without fever have a broader differential: anaemia, thyroid disease, hypoglycaemia, medication reactions, and anxiety are all common. The workup differs accordingly.

faqs.question: Can low blood sugar cause shivering and chills?
faqs.answer: Yes. Blood glucose below about 3.0 mmol/L triggers an adrenergic surge with sweating, trembling, and cold sensations. Eating 15 g of fast-acting carbohydrate resolves the episode within 15 minutes.

faqs.question: Do chills without fever go away on their own?
faqs.answer: Occasionally, if caused by a transient environmental or situational trigger. Recurrent chills without an identified and treated cause do not reliably self-resolve and warrant medical evaluation to prevent progression.

faqs.question: Can medications cause chills without fever?
faqs.answer: Yes. Amphotericin B, certain antibiotics, interferon-based therapies, and chemotherapy agents can trigger rigors by releasing cytokines. Pre-medication or dose adjustment usually prevents recurrence.

faqs.question: What does it mean when you feel cold but your temperature is normal?
faqs.answer: It means the shivering or cold sensation is arising from a non-febrile cause: most commonly anaemia, hypothyroidism, hypoglycaemia, or anxiety. A normal thermometer reading helps rule out active infection but does not eliminate early sepsis.

faqs.question: Can dehydration cause chills without fever?
faqs.answer: Mild dehydration impairs peripheral circulation and can produce a cold, clammy sensation. Severe dehydration reduces blood pressure and causes adrenergic activation with trembling. Rehydrating usually resolves the symptoms.

faqs.question: When should chills without fever be treated as an emergency?
faqs.answer: Treat as an emergency when chills accompany rapid heart rate above 100, low blood pressure, confusion, severe pallor, or recent return from a malaria-endemic region. These features suggest sepsis, severe anaemia, or malaria.

faqs.question: Can adrenal insufficiency cause chills?
faqs.answer: Yes. Low cortisol impairs heat regulation and haemodynamic responses, causing cold intolerance and episodic chills. An adrenal crisis can present with chills, hypotension, and confusion — it is a medical emergency requiring IV hydrocortisone.

metaTitle: Chills Without Fever: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Chills without fever can signal anaemia, hypothyroidism, early sepsis or anxiety. Learn the causes, diagnostic tests, and treatments. Doctor-reviewed guide.
